summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates JP Morgan Chase's disastrous Twitter Q&A session, where the bank's PR team opened themselves up to public questions using the #AskJPM hashtag. The event quickly became a viral public shaming, with critics flooding the hashtag with pointed questions about the bank's ethical failures. Fellow Occupy Wall Street activist Alexis Goldstein was highlighted as particularly effective in the takedown. The Q&A was ultimately canceled, but not before the hashtag went viral as a spectacular example of corporate PR backfiring.
key_points: JP Morgan Chase's PR team badly misjudged public sentiment by opening a Twitter Q&A
The #AskJPM hashtag was hijacked by critics and became a viral public shaming
Occupy Wall Street activists like Alexis Goldstein effectively mobilized on the platform
The event demonstrates how social media can be turned against corporate image management
money_quotes: I don't usually shill for companies but this morning I'm completely into how much of a circus my Twitter feed became
The questions asked which were tagged with #AskJPM are stunning and constitute a well-deserved public shaming of JP Morgan
Needless to say the Q&A was canceled, but not until the #AskJPM hashtag went viral in an amazing way
key_insight: Social media can transform corporate PR attempts into powerful platforms for public accountability when institutions have unaddressed ethical failures.
